books.title: The Private Concierge CV
books.subtitle: A one-page structure, the discipline behind it, and a finished example.
books.intro: How to build a private-service CV, read here, with a finished example. Download the full document, instructions and example, from the button above.

sections.html:
The person who reads your CV is not a recruiter in the ordinary sense. It is a principal, a family office, or a consultant who has read four hundred of these. They will give yours somewhere between forty and ninety seconds, and in that time they are answering one question: can this person be trusted with a life?

A private CV is therefore not a career history. It is a demonstration of judgement, and the first thing it demonstrates is what you chose to leave out. Restraint is the very first quality this world looks for, and your CV is the first place it will find it, or fail to. One page. Never two.

There are two schools, and you should know both. In private household service, especially through European agencies and for live-in or family-facing roles, a discreet, professional headshot is common and often expected. It reassures, it personalises, and its absence can read as a small omission. This is why the example here includes one.

In other contexts, chiefly direct applications in the United States, a photo is discouraged and occasionally a liability, because of anti-discrimination norms in hiring. The rule, then, is not "always" or "never", it is "read the destination". Include the photo when you apply through an agency or to a European household. Consider omitting it for a direct US application, or keep a second, photo-free version ready.

When you do include one: a real professional headshot, shoulders up, plain background, warm but composed, dressed as you would for the interview. No holiday crop, no filter, no selfie. The photo should say discretion and standard, exactly as the rest of the page does.

sections.h: The golden rule: never a name
sections.html:
Never write the name of a family, a principal, a residence, a yacht or a company you served privately. A CV that names a former private employer will be discarded by anyone serious, and rightly so, because it tells them precisely how yours will be handled next.

Instead, describe the shape of the role. "A UHNW family (private), principal residences in London and Gstaad." "A single-family office, residences in Monaco and Los Angeles." "A private luxury villa, seasonal, on the Cote d'Azur." This protects the family, and it proves the discretion you are claiming, in the same line.

Amateurs list tasks. Professionals state scope, and let numbers do the only boasting that is permitted here.

Not "managed calendars and travel". Instead: "ran the personal affairs of a family across three residences, coordinating twenty vendors and a household budget of 1.2M." Numbers, staff led, residences run, budget held, guests hosted, savings found, are the quiet proof of the size of the world you can hold. Use them, and use nothing louder.

Header. Your name, then one line beneath it: the role you are for, not only the role you had. "Private House and Estate Manager." Then a compact strip of essentials: location, mobility, languages, right to work, licences.

The line (tagline). One sentence, in italics, directly under the header. It is the only place you are permitted to speak of yourself in the round. Keep it to a breath: years of service, the kind of houses, and the discretion they required.

Profile. Three or four sentences. Who you are in a house, what you are known for, and the reassurance a principal is looking for. Calm, anticipatory, exact.

Experience. Reverse chronological. For each role: the kind of household (never the name), your title, the dates, then two to four lines. Each line starts with a verb and ends with a consequence.

Core competencies. A short, scannable block: household and estate operations, staff leadership, budgets and vendors, private travel and aviation, entertaining, security liaison, inventory, discretion. Only what you could defend, calmly, in an interview.

Languages and mobility. Stated plainly, with honest levels. In this world, mobility is not a detail, it is a qualification. If you will relocate, say so.

Education and training. Your certifications, your academy, first aid, food hygiene, driving. Relevant, not exhaustive.

References. One line: "Available on request, subject to the consent of the household." That sentence alone tells them you understand the whole profession.

No home address, no date of birth, no marital status, no photograph of anything but your face, no salary history, no reference to a named principal, and no design that draws attention to itself. Leave out every adjective you cannot prove and every word a serious person would find loud: "iconic", "passionate", "world-class". The confidence is in the restraint.

Cream or white, ink, one accent used sparingly, one clean serif and one clean sans. Generous margins. Nothing decorative. The document should feel like a calling card left on a silver tray, not a portfolio. If a choice makes it look more designed, it is the wrong choice.

sections.h: The worked example, and why it works
sections.html:
The downloadable example belongs to Camille Laurent, an imagined Private House and Estate Manager with twelve years behind her. Read it against everything above and you will see the discipline in action: every private employer is described by shape, never by name; every role leads with scope and closes on a number; the photograph is discreet and professional; the competencies are all defensible; and the references line does the quiet, knowing work it is meant to.

It is not a real person, and that is the point. It is the standard, cleaned of any single life, so that you can build your own to meet it.

One page, and it breathes. No name of any private employer, anywhere. Every experience line begins with a verb and ends with a consequence. At least one honest number in each senior role. Photograph chosen for the destination, and professional if included. Languages and mobility stated. References line present. Read aloud once, slowly, for the single typo that undoes all of it. Then, and only then, send.

books.title: The Cover Letter
books.subtitle: Your introduction, composed with restraint. Short, warm, impeccably discreet.

sections.html: I. Why this house, this family, this role. One sentence, and it must be specific. A letter that could have been sent to anyone is read as such. II. What you have carried. One or two proofs, drawn from experience, with a number if you have one. III. What you understand about the role that a stranger would not. This is where you are chosen. IV. A close without insistence. Offer yourself for a conversation, and stop.

For a private concierge position, through an agency, with a discreet reference to what the role truly asks.

Dear Ms Lawrence,

You are seeking a private concierge for a family who move often between two residences and who value, above all, that nothing about their life should require their attention twice. That is the work I know, and the work I care for.

For the past three years I have run the personal affairs of a family across four residences: their vendors, their budgets, their journeys, and the two large house projects that would have disrupted their year had they been managed less closely. They were not disrupted. I hold a household budget cleanly, I report before I am asked, and I have never once been the reason a plan had to be repeated.

What I have learned in that time is that the difficulty is rarely the request. It is being the person to whom the request can be entrusted, and remaining that person on the day everything moves at once.

I would be glad to meet the family, or to speak with you first, at whatever moment suits them. References are available with the consent of the household.

With my sincere regards,

sections.h: What to remove before you send
sections.html: · “I am writing to apply for the position of…”, they know. Begin at the second sentence. · “I believe I would be a great fit.”, belief is not evidence. Give them the evidence and let them believe. · Any adjective about yourself. Let the facts do it, or let it go. · The name of any former principal. Even here. Especially here. · An exclamation mark. There is no place for one in this world.
If you write by email, the letter is the email. Never attach it. An attachment asks the reader to work, and the first thing you are demonstrating is that with you, nothing is ever work.

books.title: The Interview Guide
books.subtitle: The questions you will be asked, and the spirit in which to meet them.

sections.html:
You will be asked ordinary questions. Almost none of them are about the answer. A family, or the consultant who protects them, is testing three things, and only three: can you be trusted with what you will inevitably come to know, will you remain calm when the day collapses, and will you be pleasant to have in the house for years.

Competence is assumed by the time you are in the room. If they doubted it, you would not be there. So do not spend the hour proving it. Spend the hour being the person they are hoping to find.

Arrive early and wait outside. Dress a level below the family, never above. Speak slightly less than you wish to. Do not fill silences: in this world, the person who is comfortable in a silence is the person in control of it. And never, at any point, be interesting at the expense of being safe.

sections.h: The questions, and what they are really asking
sections.html:
“Tell me about yourself.” They are testing restraint. Ninety seconds, no more: where you have served, what you were trusted with, and why this role. Then stop. The candidate who speaks for six minutes has already answered a different question, and badly.

“Why do you want to work for a private family?” They are listening for the word “I”. The wrong answer is about your journey, your growth, your dream. The right answer is about them: the pleasure of a life that runs without friction, the craft of making that true.

“Tell me about a difficult client or principal.” This is the discretion test, and it is the one most candidates fail. They are not asking for the story. They are watching to see whether you will tell it. Give the shape, never the detail, and never the person: “There was a situation that required a great deal of care. It was handled, and it stays with me.”

“What would you do if you saw something you were not meant to see?” The answer is short. Nothing. You would see nothing, say nothing, and it would leave no trace in you. If you are tempted to add a nuance here, do not.

“How do you handle a change of plan at eleven at night?” They are asking whether the disruption reaches your face. Describe your method calmly: the alternatives you keep ready, the suppliers who answer, the way you inform without alarming. Method reassures. Enthusiasm does not.

“What are your weaknesses?” Answer honestly and briefly, with something real and unthreatening, and say what you do about it. A polished non-answer here is more damaging than the weakness itself.

“What are your salary expectations?” Give a range, calmly, and only if asked. Then let it sit. Do not justify it, do not soften it, do not immediately offer flexibility. The person who apologises for their number invites it to be lowered.

You will be invited to ask. What you ask is itself an answer.

· “How is the household organised today, and who would I be working alongside?”, you are showing that you think in teams, and that you will not tread on a colleague’s station. · “What would make you say, in six months, that this was the right decision?”, the most valuable question you can ask anyone. · “How does the family prefer to be communicated with, and how often?”, it demonstrates that you understand the role is theirs, not yours. · “Is there anything in the way the house runs that you would like to see change?”, this is where they tell you the truth about the job.

Never ask, at a first meeting: holidays, time off, whether the role can be done remotely, or anything that begins with “would I have to…”.

Expect it, and never be surprised by it. Identity, history, references, sometimes finances and social media. It is not suspicion, it is standard, and it is the price of being admitted anywhere near a family of this kind. Prepare your history so that it is coherent, verifiable and calmly explained, including any gap. A gap that is explained without discomfort is not a problem. A gap that is defended becomes one.

Thank them once, simply. Do not oversell at the door, do not send a long letter that evening. A short, warm note the next morning, three lines, no more, is the correct gesture, and it is remembered precisely because it asks for nothing.

books.title: Approaching an Agency
books.subtitle: The first email, and the principles behind opening a door quietly.

sections.html:
Households of this standing rarely advertise. They ask a consultant they already trust, or they ask someone the family already knows. Which means the specialist agencies are not a formality to be endured: for most careers, they are the door. A consultant who believes in you will put your name forward for years, to people you will never be able to reach yourself.

So you are not applying to an agency. You are beginning a professional relationship with someone whose entire business is their judgement of people. Behave accordingly, and behave that way for a long time.

Register with several, and treat none of them casually. Five or six serious agencies, chosen for the regions and the roles you actually want. Be findable, and be consistent. Your CV, your letter and your public presence must tell the same story. A consultant will look, and a contradiction is a rejection. Never chase. One follow-up, ten days later, courteous and brief. Then leave them in peace and let your name work. Pressure reads as need, and need is the one thing this world will not hire. Answer instantly, always. When a consultant finally writes, the speed and grace of your reply is the audition. Most candidates lose the role here, before anyone has met them.

Short. Specific. Free of need. It should read as though you are already someone, quietly looking for the right house.

Subject: Private Concierge / Lifestyle Manager, Geneva, fully mobile (Claire Duval)

Dear Ms Lawrence,

I am a private concierge, based in Geneva and fully mobile, and I would like to register with you.

For the past three years I have run the personal affairs of a UHNW family across four residences: their vendors, their household budgets, their journeys, and two major house projects delivered without disruption to the family. Before that, three seasons in guest relations for private chalets and villas, and two years as a concierge in a Paris palace hotel.

I am seeking a private household, in Europe or further, where discretion and anticipation are truly valued rather than merely mentioned. My CV is attached. References are available with the consent of the household.

I would be glad to speak whenever it suits you, and I am happy to wait for the right house.

With kind regards,

+41 00 000 00 00 · claire.duval@email.com · Geneva, CH

Note the last line. “I am happy to wait for the right house” does more work than any adjective in the email. It says you are not desperate, that you have standards, and that you understand this takes time. Consultants remember the candidates who are not in a hurry.

Ten days later, once, and never again.

Dear Ms Lawrence,

A brief note, simply to remain visible to you. My situation is unchanged: available, fully mobile, and seeking the right private household rather than the next one.

I would be glad to be considered whenever something suitable comes to you, and I remain at your disposal.

With kind regards, Claire Duval

sections.h: What loses you a consultant, permanently
sections.html: · Naming a former principal or family, at any point, in any medium. · Applying to the same role through two different agencies. It will be discovered, and it ends both relationships. · Chasing. Three emails in a fortnight tells them exactly how you will behave in a household. · Declining an interview at short notice, or arriving unprepared. Their reputation is on the table, not yours. · Negotiating a salary aggressively at the first stage. There is a moment for the number. It is not the beginning. · A public social media presence that shows the life of a client, however innocently. This is the end of a career in this field.

The first role you are offered may not be the one you dreamed of. Take it if it is a good house, and be impeccable in it for two years. In private service, the second role is given by reputation, and the third is offered before you have asked. That is how this world truly works, and there is no way to skip it.

books.title: The Recruiter's View
books.subtitle: What the people who actually place you look for, and how they decide.
books.intro: Everything else in this Vault teaches you the craft. This one shows you the hiring desk: how the best agencies in private service really screen, shortlist and choose. It is drawn from recruiters who place staff in ultra-wealthy households every week, above all the Luxury Staffing podcast by Morgan & Mallet, reworked here in our own words and for you. Read it before you ever apply.

sections.h: The market now wants the all-rounder
sections.html:
The single biggest shift the recruiters describe is this: the age of the pure specialist is fading. Families increasingly want one trusted person who can cross lines, cook a little, run the house, hold the diary and the travel, and step in as a personal assistant when needed. In London this accelerated after Brexit thinned the pool of European candidates; elsewhere it is simply what a modern household finds efficient.

Present yourself, then, not as a single title but as a capable, adaptable pair of hands who can hold several of these at once, without ever letting the standard slip. Versatility, quietly stated, is now one of the most attractive things on a private CV.

sections.h: You are hired on judgement, not on paper
sections.html:
Recruiter after recruiter says the same thing: the candidate who looks perfect on paper is often not the one who fits the house. What decides it is harder to print, emotional intelligence, warmth, composure, the ability to read a room and a mood. One recruiter, herself a former private chef, says she hires on the passion she can feel in an interview long before she reaches the polished résumé.

Your CV opens the door. Who you are in the room is what walks you through it. Prepare the document, then prepare the far more important thing: the person who arrives.

Understand the machine you are entering. A serious agency will put you through a long, structured interview, often around ninety minutes, before your name ever reaches a family. They build a shortlist of only two or three, run background checks, and call your references in detail. Nothing about it is casual.

Arrive as if the first conversation is already the interview, because it is. Keep your references warm and current, and make every one of them a call you would be proud to have made on your behalf.

sections.h: The trial, and the question you did not prepare for
sections.html:
When you reach a trial or a meeting with the family, the recruiters are clear about what is watched: how you dress, how you carry yourself, and above all how you handle a question you did not see coming. Poise under the unexpected is the whole test.

You are not being asked to know everything. You are being watched to see whether you stay calm, honest and gracious in the moment when you do not. Practise that composure, because it cannot be improvised on the day.

sections.h: The map is not one market
sections.html:
Where you work changes everything. Paris is formal, credential-driven and, by several accounts, bureaucratic, with employer costs that can nearly double a gross salary. Monaco runs on tight, closed networks. Geneva rewards Swiss precision. London prizes the versatile all-rounder. Live-in and live-out, language requirements and legal frameworks all shift by city.

Know the market you are applying into before you write a single word, and let your application speak its language. The same profile is read very differently in Paris and in London.

The recruiters describe how quickly demand moves with the world. In a regional crisis, expatriate families may pause and leave while local families keep hiring, and staff willing to go where others will not can command a premium for the perceived risk. Dubai runs as a hub for a whole region's households.

The lesson is the one this academy keeps returning to: the willingness and the ability to move is not a footnote on your CV, it is one of its most valuable lines. If you can relocate, say so, and say it early.

sections.h: AI is now in the room
sections.html:
Agencies have begun using AI as a tool, to help candidates build résumés and to generate consistent, compliant reference questions, but every recruiter is emphatic that it decides nothing. A human reads the room, and a human makes the match, because character is the one thing a model cannot verify and a candidate cannot convincingly fake.

Use these tools to prepare, never to pretend. What cannot be automated, your judgement, your warmth, your discretion, is exactly what you are being hired for.

Be an adaptable all-rounder, not a single title. Let your character carry the interview, not your paper. Prepare for a long, serious vetting, and references that shine. Stay composed under the question you did not expect. Learn the market before you apply. Say yes to mobility. And treat every tool as preparation, never as disguise.

books.title: The Dictionary of Luxury
books.subtitle: The vocabulary of the world you are entering, defined plainly.

sections.html:
The Dictionary of Luxury A reference of the words, codes and gestures of the private world How to use this dictionary The private world has a language of its own. The words you choose, and the ones you avoid, signal instantly whether you belong. This dictionary is your reference for that language. Each entry gives the term, its domain, a clear definition, and, where useful, a note on how a professional actually uses it. Read it through once to build your ear. Then return to it whenever a word, a code or a gesture is unfamiliar. Over time this vocabulary will stop being something you consult, and become simply how you think and speak. This is a living reference. It will grow with new editions, and with the terms you meet in your own work. Keep it close.

One discipline above all. Never bluff a reference. If a name, a house or a vintage is unfamiliar to you, ask, or stay silent and verify later. A confident error is detected instantly in this world, and it is remembered far longer than an honest question.

À la carte Dining. Ordering individual dishes chosen separately from a menu, each priced on its own, rather than a fixed multi course menu. The opposite of a set menu. The mark of a confident host is knowing when a curated tasting menu honours the chef more than an à la carte selection.

Amuse-bouche Gastronomy. A single, bite sized morsel served before the meal, chosen by the chef rather than the guest. Literally amuses the mouth. A gesture of welcome, never charged for. Never call it a starter. The amuse-bouche is the chef's handshake, and acknowledging it graciously flatters the kitchen.

Aperitif Art De Vivre. A drink taken before a meal to open the appetite, traditionally light and dry: Champagne, a dry sherry, a light vermouth, or a Kir. From the Latin for to open. Offer the aperitif the moment a guest is seated, before menus. It signals the evening has begun and buys the kitchen time.

Atelier Fashion & Craft. A workshop or studio where a couturier, jeweller, or artisan creates by hand. In fashion, it distinguishes true made to measure craft from ready to wear production. To say a piece came from the atelier is to say it was made by hand, for one person. It is the language of the bespoke.

Background check Service. The verification of a candidate's identity, history, references and, often, finances before entry into a private household. Standard, thorough, and never negotiable. Expect it, prepare for it, and never be surprised by it. A clean, coherent history is itself a qualification in this world.

Bespoke Fashion & Service. Made entirely to individual specification, from scratch, for one client. Originally from tailoring, where cloth was spoken for. Stronger than made to measure, which adjusts an existing pattern. Reserve the word for what is truly built from nothing. Misusing bespoke for merely personalised marks the speaker as an outsider.

Black book Service. The private register of trusted contacts a professional builds over years: the florist who answers at midnight, the villa agent, the chauffeur, the doctor. Personal, confidential, and never lent. Your black book is built one favour at a time, and it is the quiet reason a principal keeps you. Guard it as you would a confidence.

Black tie Dress Codes. An evening dress code requiring a dinner jacket (tuxedo) with a black bow tie for men, and a formal gown or elegant cocktail dress for women. Less formal than white tie. If an invitation says black tie, a business suit is a quiet failure. Knowing the codes lets you guide a principal without ever correcting them.

Bottle service Hospitality. In a private club or high end venue, the purchase of a full bottle of spirits or Champagne served at a reserved table with dedicated staff, mixers, and presentation.

Boutique hotel Hospitality. A small, intimate, design led hotel, typically under a hundred rooms, prized for individuality and personal service over the uniformity of large chains. For a principal who values privacy and character, a well chosen boutique hotel often pleases more than the grandest palace property.

Butler Service. The senior male servant of a household, traditionally responsible for the dining room, the wine cellar, the pantry, and often the supervision of other staff. Today, a symbol of the highest personal service. The finest butlers are defined by anticipation and discretion, not formality. Presence without intrusion is the whole art.

Cabana Hospitality. A private, shaded structure beside a pool or beach at a luxury resort, reserved for the day, offering seclusion, dedicated service, and comfort away from the crowd.

Cellar (wine) Gastronomy. A collection of wines held and aged under controlled conditions, and the room itself. A serious cellar signals both means and genuine connoisseurship. Knowing how to speak of a cellar, its depth, its balance, its readiness, lets you converse credibly with a principal who collects.

Chauffeur Service. A professional private driver, distinguished from a taxi driver by discretion, presentation, knowledge of the principal's preferences, and seamless anticipation of the day's movements. A great chauffeur manages far more than the car: timing, privacy, the door held at the precise moment, the route chosen to avoid delay and attention.

Chief of staff (private) Service. The most senior figure in a large private household or family office: strategy, staff, properties, budgets, and the principal's entire operation. The summit of a private-service career. This is a destination, not an entry point. It is reached through years of proven trust, and never through a certificate.

Concierge Service. The professional who arranges, obtains, and solves on behalf of a guest or principal: reservations, access, logistics, the impossible request. From the French, once the keeper of a household. The concierge's true currency is the network and the reputation that opens doors. What cannot be bought can often still be arranged.

Concours d'élégance Art De Vivre. A prestigious event judging the beauty, rarity, and condition of classic and collector automobiles. A fixture of the collector and connoisseur calendar.

Couture Fashion. Short for haute couture, the creation of exclusive, made to order garments by hand, to the highest standard. In France, a legally protected term with strict criteria. Not every expensive dress is couture. The word denotes hand craft and exclusivity, not merely a high price.

Cru Gastronomy. In wine, a vineyard or estate of recognised quality, and the wine from it. Grand cru and premier cru denote the highest official classifications in regions such as Burgundy and Bordeaux. To recognise a grand cru on a list, and to know when it is drinking well, is to speak the language of the serious collector.

Crudité Gastronomy. Raw vegetables served as an appetiser, often with a dip. In fine dining, an exercise in freshness and precision rather than simplicity.

Decanting Gastronomy. Pouring wine from its bottle into a decanter, to separate it from sediment and let it breathe. Older reds especially benefit. A ritual of both function and elegance. Offer to decant a mature red without being asked. The gesture shows care and knowledge, and improves the wine the principal is about to enjoy.

Degustation Gastronomy. A tasting menu: a sequence of small, chef chosen courses designed to showcase range and progression. From the French for tasting. A degustation is an act of trust in the chef. Guiding a hesitant principal toward it, gently, is a quiet service.

Demi-bouteille Gastronomy. A half bottle of wine, 375 millilitres. Useful for a single diner, or to pair different wines across courses without excess. Suggesting half bottles lets a principal enjoy variety across a meal without waste, a considerate and refined touch.

Discretion Service. The disciplined art of holding what one knows: knowing what never needs to be mentioned, recorded, or repeated. The defining and most tested virtue of private service. Discretion is not merely silence about secrets. It is a permanent vigilance over the flow of information around the person you serve.

Egyptian cotton Art De Vivre. Long staple cotton prized for softness and durability, used in the finest bed linens and shirting. Higher thread counts are not always finer; quality of fibre matters more. Beware the myth that a higher thread count always means better linen. Fibre quality and weave matter more than the number alone.

Empty leg Travel. A private jet repositioning flight, flown with no passengers, offered at a fraction of the charter price. Timing is fixed and rarely flexible. Knowing the empty-leg market lets you offer a principal real value without ever compromising on privacy or standard.

En primeur Gastronomy. Buying wine as a future, while still in the barrel, before bottling and release. Common in Bordeaux. A way to secure sought after vintages, often at a lower price.

Entremetier Gastronomy. In a classic professional kitchen brigade, the cook responsible for vegetables, soups, and starches. Part of the brigade de cuisine system codified by Escoffier.

Escoffier Gastronomy. Auguste Escoffier, the chef who modernised and codified French haute cuisine and the professional kitchen brigade. His name is shorthand for classical culinary rigour. To reference Escoffier correctly signals genuine culinary literacy, the foundation on which modern fine dining still rests.

Estate Art De Vivre. A large property with land, often a principal country residence. In wine, the domaine where grapes are grown and wine is made, as in estate bottled.

Estate manager Service. The professional who runs one or several private residences: staff, vendors, budgets, maintenance, standards, and the guest experience. Often the head of a household team. Where a concierge arranges, an estate manager governs. Learn the difference in vocabulary before you use either title.

Etiquette Codes. The system of accepted conduct in polite and formal society: how to greet, dine, address, seat, and behave. In service, the invisible grammar that signals belonging. Etiquette is not stiffness. Its purpose is to put everyone at ease by removing all doubt about what is correct.

Family office Service. The private structure that manages an ultra-wealthy family's wealth, investments, legal affairs, philanthropy and often its household staff. A single-family office serves one family; a multi-family office serves several. Know who signs, who decides, and who merely relays. In many houses, the family office, not the family, is who truly hires you.

Fine dining brigade Gastronomy. The organised hierarchy of a professional kitchen, from the chef de cuisine down through the sous chef and the chefs de partie, each with a defined station. Codified by Escoffier. Understanding the brigade lets you speak correctly with a kitchen and know who to address for what. Precision of address signals respect and knowledge.

Flute Gastronomy. A tall, narrow glass for Champagne and sparkling wine, designed to preserve the bubbles. Increasingly, connoisseurs prefer a white wine glass to reveal aroma. Know that serious Champagne is now often served in a tulip or white wine glass, not a flute, to release its full aroma. A quiet mark of expertise.

Foie gras Gastronomy. The rich, delicate liver of a specially fattened duck or goose, a classic of French haute cuisine, served seared or as a terrine. Legally restricted in some regions. Be aware that foie gras is controversial and banned or restricted in some places. Read the table and the setting before assuming it is welcome.

Gala Hospitality. A grand, formal social event, often for charity, typically black or white tie, with dinner, entertainment, and a programme. A key fixture of the philanthropic season.

Garde manger Gastronomy. The kitchen station and cook responsible for cold dishes: charcuterie, salads, terrines, and the pantry. From the French, to keep to eat.

Grand cru Gastronomy. The highest official vineyard classification in regions such as Burgundy, Alsace, and Champagne, denoting exceptional and consistent quality of terroir.

Gratuity Service. A tip, given for good service. In the private world, norms vary widely by country and setting; in some, a discreet fixed arrangement replaces individual tipping entirely. Learn the tipping norms of each country you operate in. Getting it wrong, high or low, quietly signals unfamiliarity with the setting.

Haute couture Fashion. The pinnacle of fashion: exclusive, hand made, made to order garments meeting strict standards. In France, a label legally restricted to approved houses by the Chambre Syndicale. Only a small number of houses may legally use haute couture in France. It is a protected designation, not a loose compliment.

Haute horlogerie Horlogerie. The highest level of watchmaking: complex movements, hand finishing, and rare complications, made in small numbers by the most prestigious houses. In haute horlogerie, value lies in the movement and finishing, the craft unseen on the wrist, not merely the name on the dial.

Hospitality Service. The art and business of welcoming and caring for guests. At the highest level, the seamless anticipation of needs so that a guest feels effortlessly at home.

Household budget Service. The annual and monthly budget of a private residence: staff, vendors, maintenance, provisions, travel. Managed with precision, reported without being asked. Handling a household budget cleanly is the fastest way to become indispensable, and mishandling it the fastest way to leave.

Household staff Service. The team serving a private residence: house manager, butler, housekeeper, chef, nanny, chauffeur, gardener, security. Each with a defined place, and a hierarchy to be respected. Know who does what, and never step across a colleague's station without being asked. Harmony among staff is itself a service to the principal.

Iconic Codes. Widely overused, and therefore powerful when withheld. Truly iconic denotes an object or design so recognisable it defines its category. Use sparingly, or lose its force. Restraint with superlatives is itself a code. The professional who calls few things iconic is believed when they finally do.

In-villa dining Hospitality. A private meal prepared and served within a guest's villa or suite by dedicated staff, offering total privacy and a bespoke menu away from the restaurant. For a principal guarding privacy, in-villa dining is often preferred to the finest restaurant. Anticipate and offer it before it is requested.

Jeroboam Gastronomy. A large format wine bottle, equivalent to three or four standard bottles depending on region. Large formats age more gracefully and make an occasion of a table.

Jet lag protocol Service. The anticipatory management of a principal's arrival across time zones: adjusted meals, light, rest, and schedule to ease the body's adjustment. A hallmark of thoughtful private travel. Anticipating jet lag, quietly adjusting the first day's plans and meals, protects the principal's peace before they have felt the strain.
Kir Gastronomy. A classic French aperitif of white wine with a touch of crème de cassis (blackcurrant liqueur). Kir Royale uses Champagne in place of still wine.
Livery Service. The distinctive uniform worn by household staff or a chauffeur, historically in a family's colours. A mark of a formal, traditional household.

Made to measure Fashion. A garment made by adjusting an existing base pattern to a client's measurements. More personal than ready to wear, but distinct from bespoke, which is built from scratch. Know the ladder: ready to wear, then made to measure, then bespoke. Using the terms precisely marks genuine familiarity.

Magnum Gastronomy. A wine bottle holding 1.5 litres, the equivalent of two standard bottles. Prized because wine ages more slowly and elegantly in the larger format. Suggesting a magnum for a table of several guests is both practical and festive, and signals an eye for the occasion.

Maître d'hôtel Hospitality. The head of the dining room, responsible for reservations, seating, service, and the guest experience. Often shortened to maître d'. The public face of a fine restaurant. A relationship with a great maître d' is worth more than any booking system. Cultivate it with courtesy and it opens tables others cannot reach.

Michelin star Gastronomy. A distinction awarded by the Michelin Guide for culinary excellence, from one star (very good) to three (worth a special journey). The most recognised mark in fine dining. Know that one star is a genuine achievement and three is exceptionally rare. Speak of stars accurately, not loosely.

Mise en place Gastronomy. Everything in its place: the disciplined preparation and arrangement of all ingredients and tools before service begins. A principle of readiness that extends well beyond the kitchen. Mise en place is a philosophy for all service: everything anticipated and ready, so the moment itself unfolds without friction.

Monogram Art De Vivre. A personal emblem of initials, applied to linens, luggage, stationery, or shirts. A discreet, traditional mark of ownership and refinement, best kept subtle. The elegance of a monogram lies in its subtlety. Tone on tone, small, and well placed says more than something large and loud.

NDA Service. A non-disclosure agreement, signed before or upon entering a private household. It binds you to silence about everything you see, hear and learn, often permanently. An NDA is not a formality, it is the floor. The professionals worth hiring behave as though one existed even where none was signed.

Omakase Gastronomy. A Japanese dining style meaning I leave it to you: the chef selects and serves a personalised sequence, often at the counter. An act of trust in the chef's judgement. Omakase rewards a guest who surrenders control to the chef. Encouraging a principal to relax into it is a gentle, knowing service.

Orangerie Art De Vivre. A grand glasshouse or garden room, historically for wintering citrus trees, now often an elegant space for events and dining on a great estate.

Palace hotel Hospitality. In France, an official distinction above five stars, awarded to a small number of exceptional hotels. More broadly, a grand hotel of the highest standing and history. In France, palace is an official rank above five stars, not a loose term. Knowing this distinguishes the informed from the impressed.

Patina Art De Vivre. The soft sheen and character that age gives to fine leather, wood, silver, or a watch. Prized as evidence of quality and history, never to be polished away. Old money treasures patina; it is proof of quality that has endured. To over restore an heirloom is to erase its story.

Pied-à-terre Art De Vivre. A small secondary residence kept in a city for occasional use. From the French, foot on the ground. A convenience of the mobile and the well established.

Principal Service. The person one serves, in a household or private capacity. The preferred, respectful term over boss, employer, or client, conveying the seriousness and dignity of the relationship. Principal is the correct register in the private household. Using it naturally signals that you understand the world you work in.

Private aviation Travel. Travel by private jet or chartered aircraft, offering privacy, flexibility, and time. Terms such as fractional ownership, charter, and empty leg denote different ways of access. Learn the vocabulary of private aviation: charter, fractional, empty leg. Fluency lets you manage a principal's travel with quiet confidence.

Provenance Art & Collecting. The documented history of ownership of an artwork, antique, or fine object. Strong provenance authenticates, adds value, and reassures. A gap in provenance raises questions. In art and collecting, provenance is everything: it proves authenticity and protects value. Always ask for it before advising a purchase.

Ready-to-wear Fashion. Prêt-à-porter: garments made in standard sizes for immediate purchase, as opposed to made to measure or bespoke. The foundation of most luxury fashion houses' business.

Reserve Gastronomy. On a wine or spirit label, a term suggesting higher quality or longer ageing. In some regions strictly regulated, in others merely marketing. Know which is which. Reserve means something precise in Rioja and little in other places. Do not assume the word alone guarantees quality.

Ritz-Carlton Hospitality. A byword for a standard of gracious, anticipatory hospitality. Its founding philosophy, ladies and gentlemen serving ladies and gentlemen, shaped modern luxury service. The Ritz-Carlton credo, that service is dignified on both sides, is a useful model: you serve from a place of professional pride, never servility.

Rota Service. The staff schedule of a private household: who is on duty, when, and where, including rotations, cover, and time off. Often complex across several residences. A well-kept rota is invisible and a badly kept one is felt by everyone. Learn to read one before you presume to build one.

Savoir-faire Codes. Literally, knowing how to do: expertise, skill, and the quiet competence to handle any situation gracefully. A prized and untranslatable quality in luxury craft and service. Savoir-faire is competence made to look effortless. It is shown, never announced.

Savoir-vivre Codes. Literally, knowing how to live: the grace, manners, and ease of someone at home in refined society. The social counterpart to savoir-faire. Savoir-vivre is the art of putting others at ease. Your own composure and courtesy quietly grant it to everyone around you.

Security detail Service. The close protection team around a principal and their family. Movements, routes, guests and schedules are coordinated with them, always. Never plan a movement without the detail. A beautiful itinerary that ignores security is not a service, it is a risk.

Silver service Service. A formal serving technique in which the server transfers food from a platter to the guest's plate using a spoon and fork held in one hand. A mark of trained, traditional service. Silver service is a genuine skill that takes practice. Where it is expected, competence is assumed and clumsiness is instantly visible.

Sommelier Gastronomy. The wine specialist of a fine restaurant, responsible for the list, pairings, and guidance. A great sommelier reads the guest as carefully as the cellar. Trust and cultivate the sommelier. Asking their guidance, rather than performing knowledge, often yields the finest and best judged pairing.

Staff meal Service. The meal taken by household staff, distinct from the family's table. Its quality and timing say a great deal about how a house treats those who serve it. Notice it in any house you enter. It will tell you more about the family than any interview ever will.

Suite Hospitality. A hotel accommodation of multiple connected rooms, from a junior suite to a presidential or royal suite. The tier and naming signal a property's finest offering. Know a property's suite hierarchy before booking. Placing a principal in the right suite, for the right occasion, is a considered act.

Terroir Gastronomy. The complete natural environment, soil, climate, slope, that gives a wine or produce its distinctive character. A concept at the heart of French ideas of quality and place. To speak of terroir is to understand that the finest things are of a place. It signals depth beyond brand names.

Thread count Art De Vivre. The number of threads per square inch in a fabric, often cited for bed linen. A useful but overrated measure; fibre quality and weave matter more than a high number alone. Do not be seduced by a high thread count. A finer fibre at a moderate count often makes the superior sheet.

Tourbillon Horlogerie. A watch complication, a rotating cage for the escapement, invented to counter the effect of gravity on accuracy. Today, chiefly a demonstration of watchmaking mastery. The tourbillon is prized as a feat of craft more than for practical gain. Admire it as artistry, and say so accurately.

Trousseau Art De Vivre. Traditionally, the fine linens, clothing, and household goods a bride brought to a marriage. Now, more broadly, a curated collection of the finest personal or household items.

Truffle Gastronomy. A rare, intensely aromatic subterranean fungus, white (Alba) or black (Périgord), among the most prized and costly ingredients in gastronomy. Shaved fresh, never cooked hard. White Alba truffle is seasonal and extraordinary. Knowing the season and handling, shaved fresh at the table, marks true familiarity.

Turndown service Hospitality. The evening preparation of a hotel room for sleep: bed opened, curtains drawn, lights softened, a small touch left. A quiet ritual of anticipatory care. Turndown is anticipation made visible: the room readied for rest before the guest thinks to want it. Study its logic; apply it everywhere.

Valet Service. A personal male attendant responsible for a gentleman's wardrobe, dressing, and personal effects. Also, the service of parking a guest's car. Context distinguishes the two.

Vintage Gastronomy. The year a wine's grapes were harvested, and, more broadly, wine from a single specified year. A great vintage denotes a year of exceptional growing conditions. Knowing the strong and weak vintages of the major regions lets you guide a wine choice with genuine, quiet authority.

White glove Service. Denoting service of the highest care and attention to detail, from the gloves once worn to handle fine objects. Now shorthand for premium, meticulous, personal handling. White glove is a promise of meticulous care. Use the phrase only where you can truly deliver the standard it implies.

White tie Dress Codes. The most formal evening dress code: for men, a black tailcoat, white waistcoat, and white bow tie; for women, a full length formal gown. Above black tie in formality. White tie is rare and exacting. Recognising it on an invitation, and preparing a principal correctly, prevents a conspicuous and memorable error.

Yacht (charter vs ownership) Travel. A luxury vessel, accessed by ownership or by charter (hire, usually by the week, with crew). Terms such as tender, flag, and charter broker denote the specialised world around it. Learn the difference between owning and chartering, and the role of the broker and captain. It lets you manage a principal's time on the water with confidence.

Zero proof Gastronomy. The contemporary term for sophisticated non alcoholic drinks, crafted with the same care as cocktails. Offering an elegant zero proof option is now a mark of thoughtful hospitality. Always have a considered zero proof option ready. Offering it, unprompted and without fuss, quietly respects every guest's choice.

books.title: The Household Operations Toolkit
books.subtitle: The real working documents of the private-service profession, ready to use, ready to carry into your first house.
books.intro: A confidential resource of Maison Amarine. These are the instruments a professional runs a household with. Learn them here, adapt them to your house, and you will look like someone who has done this for years, from your very first week.

sections.html:
A great house is not run on memory or charm. It is run on quiet systems that make excellence repeatable and make you, over time, impossible to replace. The tools in this kit are those systems.

Two rules govern all of them. First, discretion: every one of these documents contains private information, so it is kept secure, never left open, never photographed, never taken home, and never discussed. Second, the manual survives you: you build these so that if you were gone tomorrow, the house would keep running. Paradoxically, that is exactly what makes a household refuse to let you go.

What follows are not descriptions of templates. They are the templates. Each one gives you the sections to build, the fields to name, the cadence that keeps it current, the ways it commonly fails, and a checklist you can hold it against. Copy the structure, fill it with the truth of your own house, and keep it current, because a tool that is out of date is worse than no tool at all.

One note on scope. These instruments record and organise; they do not decide. Anything touching employment terms, tax, insurance cover or contracts belongs to the family office and the family's professional advisers, and nothing in this kit is legal, tax or financial advice.

The Household Manual is the book that runs the house when you are not in it. It is written for the person who arrives at seven in the morning and has never been here before: a new housekeeper, a covering estate manager, a family member who has not stayed here in two years. When it does not exist, every answer lives in one person's head and the house stops the day that person is ill.

What it is for. It converts your knowledge into property of the house. It shortens onboarding from a month to a day, settles disagreements about standards without anyone having to remember who said what, and gives you a page to hand over when a principal asks a question.

The template, section by section. Ten parts, in this order. The order matters, because the first page is the one someone reads in a hurry.

1. The emergency page. Page one, printed as well as stored. Address as emergency services need to hear it, nearest cross street, nearest emergency department with drive time, poison control, family physician, security lead and after hours number, on call electrician, plumber and HVAC contractor, and the outside assembly point. Names and numbers only, no decisions.

2. The house at a glance. Year built, square footage, bedrooms and baths, utility account numbers and account holder of record, internet provider and account, refuse collection days, mail and courier handling, building management if any, and the property boundaries with anything shared noted plainly.

3. Access and security basics. Which doors are used by whom, delivery protocol, guest arrival protocol, camera coverage and who may view it, how visitors are logged. Codes and passwords are not written here. Record only where the credential lives, for example "alarm code: family password manager, vault House, access granted by the estate manager". A manual with live codes becomes a security incident the moment it is photographed.

4. Systems and shut offs. The section that saves a floor. Per system: location, make and model, serial number, install date, service contractor, service interval, warranty expiry. Cover the main water shut off with a photograph and a written route to it, isolation valves per bathroom and appliance, gas shut off, electrical panels with a legible photograph of the labelled directory, generator with fuel source and test schedule, irrigation, pool plant, HVAC zones and filter sizes, water heater, alarm panel.

5. Room by room standards. One block per room: how the room is left when unoccupied, lighting setting, target temperature, window treatment position, linens and their rotation, products permitted and forbidden on each surface, fragile items and how they are handled, and a dated photograph of the room in its correct state. The photograph settles more arguments than any paragraph.

6. Routines. Three lists. Daily: opening the house, mail, plants, pool skim, laundry, evening close down. Weekly: linen change, deep clean by zone, vehicle wash, filter check. Seasonal and annual: HVAC service spring and fall, gutters, wardrobe changeover, window cleaning, chimney, tree work, freeze or storm preparation, holiday setup, each with the date it begins.

7. The people. Household roles with hours, reporting lines, who authorises spending, who signs for deliveries, who speaks to contractors. Personal details of family members belong in the Principal's Book, not here.

8. Vendors in service. The suppliers currently engaged, cross referenced to the Vendor Black Book, with renewal dates and the scope each covers.

9. Incident conduct. What a member of staff does in the first five minutes of a leak, an outage, a broken window or an alarm activation: stabilise, notify, photograph, then repair. Cross reference the Emergency and Security Protocol for anything involving people.

10. Version table. Last page. Version number, date, who edited, what changed.

How to keep it alive. Edit the same day anything changes; review in full quarterly. One owner holds edit rights, usually you, and everyone else suggests. It lives in the household's approved storage with restricted access, plus one printed binder in the service area for the day the network is down. Back it up monthly. It never goes home with you.

How it fails. It is written once and never touched, so the contractor listed retired two years ago. It contains alarm codes and is then emailed to a vendor. It describes standards in adjectives rather than measurements, so "immaculate" means five things to five people. Or it is treated as your private asset and leaves with you.

Your checklist. The emergency page is page one and printed. Every shut off has a photograph and a written route. Every room has a standards block and a dated photograph. No live codes appear anywhere. Daily, weekly and seasonal routines are populated with dates. A competent stranger could run the house tomorrow without calling you.

The Principal's Book is the record of one family's taste, held so that their world arrives already correct. Old service households simply call it the book. Only you and your successor ever read it. Without it, service becomes a series of polite questions, and a principal asked for the ninth time how they take their coffee has already begun to feel unknown in their own home.

What it is for. It moves you from reacting to anticipating, and it protects the family from your absence: the preference learned in month three survives your departure. Everything in it is a fact of preference or a practical detail. Nothing in it is an opinion, a judgement or a note about anyone's character.

The template, section by section. One page per principal, then a shorter page per family member and regular guest. Use fields, not prose, so an entry is found in four seconds.

1. Identity and practical details. Full legal name as it appears on documents, preferred form of address, date of birth, mobile numbers with the one they actually answer marked, email addresses and which is monitored by an assistant, and the gatekeeper who controls access.

2. Food and drink. The most used page. Coffee: method, roast, milk or none, temperature, cup. Tea: variety, steep time, milk or lemon. Water: still or sparkling, brand, chilled or room, glass or bottle. Then weekday and weekend breakfast, foods loved, foods refused, dietary pattern, portion size, meal timings, wines kept, wines never served, and the standing breakfast order at each regular hotel.

3. Medical and allergy. Allergies and intolerances with severity and reaction, current medications with prescribing physician and pharmacy, blood type where the family has chosen to record it, physician, dentist, and any medication or device that must travel. Mark this section restricted. You hold it so care can be arranged quickly; you never make a clinical judgement, you call the physician or emergency services.

4. Comfort and the house. Bedroom temperature and night setting, side of the bed, mattress and pillow specification with supplier and model, sheet fabric, laundry detergent, bathroom amenities by brand and size, towel type and folding, house scent and any fragrance forbidden, curtains drawn or open on waking, and which papers are placed out and where.

5. Wardrobe, sizes and measurements. Clothing sizes by brand, since they differ; shoe size with width; collar and sleeve; inseam and waist; ring size; preferred fabrics and colours; what is dry cleaned versus hand washed; the tailor, cobbler and dry cleaner with account numbers; and the packing standard, meaning what goes in the bag regardless of the trip.

6. Travel documents and expiries. A table with one row per document: type, holder, number, issuing authority, issue date, expiry date, and where the original is held. Cover passports, visas with entry conditions, trusted traveller memberships, driving licences and travel insurance policy numbers. Set a reminder nine months before every passport expiry, since many countries refuse entry inside six months of validity. Confirm entry requirements with the airline or consulate before travel; this is a record, not an authority.

7. Travel habits. Airlines and loyalty numbers, seat preference down to the row, hotels and the specific suite numbers returned to, the contact used at each, car service preferences and the route from each airport, and the ritual on landing.

8. People and dates. Family members and how each is addressed, godchildren, close friends, key advisers, and a calendar of birthdays, anniversaries and annual events. Record the repeated florist order including stem count and colour.

9. What is never written here. No account numbers, passwords, safe combinations or alarm codes. No medical opinions. Nothing about anyone's marriage, mood, finances or character, and nothing about other staff. Assume you will one day be asked to hand it over, and write every line so that moment is uneventful.

10. Access. Name who may open it, usually the principal, the estate manager and a named successor, where it is stored, and how access is granted and revoked when someone leaves.

How to keep it alive. Add the same evening you observe something, then act on it the next morning. Do not build it by interrogation: three questions in a week is curiosity, thirty is surveillance. Review monthly for expiries. It lives encrypted in approved storage with named access, never in a personal cloud.

How it fails. It drifts into commentary and becomes a liability. It is stored casually and becomes a breach. It is built by asking rather than noticing. Or it goes unrevised, so the passport in it expired last spring.

Your checklist. Every entry is a preference or a practical fact. The medical section is restricted and dated. Every travel document has an expiry and a reminder. Sizes are recorded by brand. Access is named and revocable. You could hand it to your successor tomorrow without editing a line first.

The Rota is the written answer to one question: at any hour of any day, who is responsible for this house. It is read by every member of staff and by whoever covers for you. A house without one runs on last minute phone calls, days off that keep moving, and the discovery at eight on a Friday evening that nobody is on for the weekend.

What it is for. It guarantees coverage, protects rest, and produces the record you need when hours are questioned months later. It also makes your value visible: a principal who never wonders whether someone is on duty has stopped thinking about staffing entirely.

The template, section by section. A grid with supporting tabs. A spreadsheet is enough and is easier to hand over than any application.

1. The staff register. One row per person: name, role, contracted hours per week, standard days off, start date, mobile, emergency contact, languages, whether insured on household vehicles, and any certification with its expiry. Note who holds keys, alarm credentials and vehicle access, since that determines who can open the house alone.

2. The coverage grid. The core sheet. Columns are the seven days; rows are the shift bands you use, typically early at six to fourteen hundred, day at nine to seventeen, evening at fourteen to twenty two, and overnight where the house carries it. Write the name in each cell, not the role. Add a bottom row per day reading duty lead, naming the one person accountable. Every cell is filled or marked no cover, and no cover is a decision made in advance rather than a gap discovered later.

3. Position requirements. The minimum staffing for each state of the house: family in residence, family away, guests staying, formal event. For example, family in residence requires one housekeeper and one duty lead from seven to twenty one hundred; family away requires a daily property check of one hour.

4. Rotations. Where staff move between residences or follow the family, record the pattern, for example two weeks on and one week off. Include travel days, who is in transit and therefore unavailable, and how handover happens: a written note covering open items, deliveries expected and anything unresolved.

5. Cover and absence. A named first cover and second cover for every role, agreed in advance and known to the person covering. Record the notice expected for planned absence, how sickness is reported and to whom, and the relief agency used when the household cannot cover internally. A rota that only works when everyone is healthy is not a rota.

6. Leave. One row per person: entitlement, taken, booked, remaining, plus blackout periods such as the two weeks around the summer residence move. Record the date each request was made and the date it was approved. Entitlement and its treatment are set by the family office and by applicable employment rules, which differ by state. Confirm with them; nothing here is legal advice.

7. Hours and overtime. A weekly log per person: scheduled hours, actual hours, variance, and the reason for any variance such as a late dinner service. Note the household's rule for how additional hours are approved and by whom, before they are worked, and submit the log the same day each week to whoever handles payroll. Wage and hour rules vary by jurisdiction; your job is an accurate record.

8. Peak periods. A forward calendar of the dates that break a normal rota: arrival and departure at each residence, holidays hosted, an annual event, a renovation window, school holidays. Staff these eight to twelve weeks ahead, not in the week itself.

9. The published rota. The extract that goes on the service area wall and into the team's hands. Publish at least four weeks ahead, in the same place, at the same time, in the same format. Changes after publication are exceptional and are told to the person affected by you personally before they appear on the grid.

How to keep it alive. Update daily for actuals, publish the next four weeks every Monday, review leave and hours weekly, rebuild the peak calendar quarterly. You hold edit rights; the team holds read access. It lives in shared storage with the current fortnight printed in the service area, and completed weeks are archived.

How it fails. Days off are promised then quietly taken back, which costs the team's trust faster than anything else. Roles are written into cells instead of names, so nobody feels responsible. Cover is improvised on the day, or the person covering was never told. Or you fill every gap yourself, which looks like dedication for six weeks and then ends a career.

Your checklist. Every shift band on every day names a person or is marked no cover. Every role has a named first and second cover. The next four weeks are published. Leave balances are current and blackouts marked. Last week's hours log is complete and submitted. The rota still works if any one person is absent tomorrow.

The Budget and Expense Log records what the household spends, against what was planned, with a receipt for every line. It is read by the principal, by the family office or accountant, and occasionally by an auditor. Handled cleanly it is the fastest route to being trusted with more. Handled loosely it ends positions, because money is the one area where a good explanation always arrives too late.

What it is for. It lets the household see its own spending before someone else points it out, gives you a defensible answer to any question about any sum, and turns you into the person who reports rather than the person who is asked. You are a record keeper here: tax treatment and anything resembling accounting judgement belongs to the family's advisers, and nothing in this template is tax or legal advice.

The template, section by section. A workbook with the tabs below. Keep the structure boring and consistent, because consistency is what makes twelve months comparable.

1. The chart of categories. Agree it once with the family office and never invent categories mid year. A workable set: household payroll; provisions; cleaning and supplies; maintenance and repairs; utilities; landscape and pool; vehicles; vendors under contract; entertaining; travel; technology; gifts and flowers; petty cash; capital items above the household's threshold. Give each a code, for example MTN for maintenance.

2. The annual budget. One row per category with an annual figure and a monthly phasing, since spending is not flat: landscape peaks in spring, utilities in summer or winter. Build year one from last year's actuals where they exist and from vendor contracts where they do not, and mark every figure contracted, estimated or historic so everyone knows which numbers are firm.

3. The expense log. The working sheet. One row per transaction: date, vendor, plain language description, category code, amount, payment method with the last four digits of the card, who authorised it, receipt reference, notes. Log the same day. A week of undocumented spending takes four hours to reconstruct and never reconstructs perfectly.

4. Planned against actual. A monthly summary with four columns per category: budget, actual, variance in currency, variance as a percentage. Set an attention threshold, commonly ten per cent on a category, and write one line beside every flagged row. "Maintenance over by two thousand one hundred: emergency water heater replacement on the twelfth, approved by the family office" closes a question before it is asked.

5. Approval thresholds. Write down what you may spend without asking, what needs one approval, and what needs written approval, then obey it exactly. A common shape: routine spending inside budget at your discretion up to a set amount; above it, approval by the family office in advance; outside budget, written approval by the principal; three written quotes above a defined level. Record approver and date every time, and never split an invoice to stay under a threshold.

6. Petty cash and the float. Record the float amount, where it is held and who holds it, and reconcile weekly on a fixed day. Columns: date, amount out, purpose, receipt attached, balance remaining, initials. Balance plus receipts equals the float, every week, to the cent. If it does not, you say so the same day rather than the next month.

7. Receipts. Every transaction has an image named to a fixed convention such as 2026-03-14_MTN_Hollis-Plumbing_412.80.pdf, filed by month in approved storage. Photograph paper receipts the day they arrive, since thermal paper fades within months. Retain for the period the family office specifies.

8. The monthly close. A fixed routine on a fixed date, typically the third working day. Reconcile every card statement line to the log, confirm the petty cash balance, chase missing receipts, confirm every vendor invoice was received and paid, then lock the month so nobody edits it afterwards.

9. The one page report. What you actually send. Top: total spent this month, total against budget year to date, variance. Middle: the categories that moved, each with its one line explanation. Bottom: anything large in the next sixty days, any contract due for renewal, and any decision you need. One page, same format, same date, whether or not anyone asked.

How to keep it alive. Log daily, reconcile petty cash weekly, close monthly on a fixed date, rebuild the budget with the family office two months before the year starts. You hold edit rights on the log; the family office holds the budget. It lives in approved storage with automatic backup, never on a personal device.

How it fails. Receipts collect in a drawer and are reconciled quarterly, by which point half are illegible. Personal and household spending touch the same card even once. A category is invented mid year and comparison dies. Or a report is delayed because the numbers are bad, which converts a manageable variance into a question about your judgement.

Your checklist. Every transaction in the last thirty days has a receipt image and a category code. Petty cash reconciled this week to the cent. Approval thresholds are written down and were followed without exception. Last month is closed and locked. The one page report went out on the agreed date. You could answer any question about any line within two minutes.

The Travel Brief carries a principal from one door to another without a single question being necessary. It is read by the principal, by whoever travels with them, and by the person left holding the house. Without it, a delayed aircraft becomes four phone calls, a driver waits at the wrong terminal, and the family learns that the smoothness they experience depends on luck.

What it is for. It removes decisions from the day of travel: everything that can be settled in advance is settled in advance, so the only things left are the ones nobody could predict. It also protects you, because a brief circulated two days ahead means every change was visible, agreed and dated.

The template, section by section. One document per trip, produced at least three days before departure and longer for international travel.

1. The cover block. Trip name, dates, travellers by name with the lead marked, purpose in one line, your name and mobile as coordinating contact, and a version number with date and time. Time stamp every reissue, because on a moving day three copies circulate at once and only the newest is safe.

2. The minute by minute itinerary. The heart of it. One row per movement: time in local time with the origin zone alongside, event, location with full address, who is responsible, confirmation number. Write it granularly. "0612 car departs residence, Marcus, plate ending 419. 0648 arrive FBO Signature, 4200 Aviation Way, ask for Dana. 0715 wheels up. 1042 wheels down local." Show buffers explicitly and mark the two or three moments where a delay would cascade.

3. Aviation or commercial detail. For private lift: operator, aircraft type, tail number, crew names with the captain's mobile, FBO at each end with address and telephone, catering order and when it was placed, luggage limits, handling agent. Treat the tail number as sensitive and keep it out of anything that circulates widely. For commercial: airline, flight numbers, record locator, seats, meet and assist contact.

4. Ground and accommodation. Each car with driver name, mobile, vehicle and plate, and a pickup point described precisely rather than by building name. Each property with full address, reservation name and number, suite number, check in arrangement, the manager who knows the family, and the arrival preferences drawn from the Principal's Book.

5. Documents. A checklist with a tick per traveller: passport with expiry and confirmation of at least six months validity where required, visa with reference, trusted traveller membership, driving licence, travel insurance policy number and its emergency line, and copies stored separately from the originals. Confirm current entry requirements with the airline or consulate close to departure; nothing here is immigration advice.

6. Luggage and provisioning. A numbered manifest of bags with description and owner, so a missing piece is identified in seconds. Note what travels in the cabin, the medication bag and who carries it, adapters for the destination, currency, and anything shipped ahead with its tracking number.

7. Time zones and the first day. State the time difference and direction of travel, then plan the first day around it: a light arrival meal, a first appointment later than usual where the schedule allows, daylight exposure in the afternoon, and no significant decisions on the evening of a long eastward flight. Nobody thanks you for this and everybody feels it.

8. Local contacts and medical. Per destination: nearest emergency department with address and drive time; a clinic or physician the family's advisers have approved; a pharmacy near the residence with opening hours; the local security contact; the consulate emergency number; and the local emergency services number, which is not the same everywhere. Arranging care is coordination, not medical judgement.

9. Contingencies. Written before the day, not during it. If the aircraft goes technical, the named alternative operator and number. If weather closes the airport, the alternate airfield and drive time. If a bag is lost, what is duplicated at destination. If a traveller falls ill, who is called first. Name who decides in each case, because the plan matters less than knowing that.

10. Distribution list. Who receives which version. The principal usually gets a one page extract with times, addresses and contacts. Crew, drivers and hosts receive only the part that concerns them. Never circulate the whole document to a vendor.

How to keep it alive. Draft at booking, refine daily as confirmations arrive, freeze forty eight hours out, reissue only for material change and always with a new version number. Reconfirm every ground element twenty four hours before. Keep an offline copy on your phone, because the moment you need it most is often the moment you have no signal.

How it fails. Times are written without zones. The driver is given the airport name rather than the FBO address, and the two are twenty minutes apart. A passport sits inside its six month window and nobody checked. Or the full brief, tail number included, is forwarded to a supplier who never needed it.

Your checklist. Every time carries a zone. Every address is a street address, not a name. Every document has an expiry that clears the trip. Every ground element was reconfirmed inside twenty four hours. There is a written plan B for aircraft, weather, luggage and illness. The version number is on page one and the principal's copy is one page.

The Emergency and Security Protocol is the page you hand to a frightened person so they act correctly. It is read by every member of staff, by the family, and possibly by a house sitter who has been here four hours. It exists because in a real incident nobody reasons well, and the quality of the next ten minutes depends on what was decided months earlier, calmly, in writing.

What it is for. It converts panic into sequence, and it protects the family from well meaning improvisation: the housekeeper who moves an injured person, the gardener who admits a stranger because they said they were expected.

The template, section by section. Two documents: a laminated one page card posted in the service area and kitchen and carried in every vehicle, and the full protocol behind it. The card holds numbers and first actions only.

1. The escalation tree. The spine of the document. Per event type, the order of calls with a name, a role and a number: emergency services first where life or property is at risk, then the security lead, the estate manager, the family office, then the principal. Write the rule for when the principal is called directly, and the rule for a call unanswered within two minutes, which is that you move to the next name. Emergency services are never delayed to seek permission.

2. Fire. Evacuate first, using the primary route and the named alternative from each floor. Call emergency services from outside. Do not re enter. Extinguishers are used only on a contained fire smaller than a wastebasket and only with the exit behind you. Record the assembly point, the head count with a named counter, where pets are collected, and the fire panel location with how to read the zone in alarm.

3. Water. Escaping water is the most common expensive incident in a large house. Shut off at the nearest isolation valve or the main, photographed in the Household Manual. Cut power to the area if water is near electrics. Move and protect art, rugs and electronics, photograph before anything moves further, call the plumber on the emergency list, notify the estate manager, and begin drying within hours, because the secondary damage is worse than the leak.

4. Intrusion and unknown persons. Distinguish clearly. An intruder inside means you do not investigate: move to a safe location, lock it, call emergency services, then the security lead. An unknown person at the gate is handled with a script: you do not confirm whether anyone is home, you do not confirm names, you take a name and a purpose, and you log it with a time and camera reference. Record the safe room, panic button locations, and any duress word agreed.

5. Medical event. Call emergency services first. Do not move the person unless they are in immediate danger. Send someone to the gate to meet the ambulance. Bring the medical page from the Principal's Book, meaning allergies, medications and physician, and hand it to the paramedics, then notify per the escalation tree. Record which staff hold current first aid certification and when it expires. You provide information and access, not clinical decisions.

6. Press, cameras and enquiries at the gate. One rule: nobody in the household comments on anything, ever, to anyone. The script is a single sentence declining and directing the enquiry to the family's representative, whose name and number sit here. Nothing is confirmed, including whether anyone is home. Log the encounter with time, description and any vehicle plate, and notify the security lead the same hour.
