The three levers are authority, urgency and plausibility: a message appearing to come from the principal, a deadline that removes time to think, and enough true detail to feel real. That detail is not evidence of legitimacy, it is evidence of research. The professional reflex is the opposite of the helpful one: slow down precisely when a message tells you to hurry.

Payment instructions, and the rule that never bends. Invoice fraud is among the most common serious losses in private households, and it works simply: an attacker intercepts or imitates a supplier's correspondence and sends new bank details for a genuine invoice. Every change of payment details is verified by voice, on a number from your own records, never a number in the message and never by replying to the email. Verify the first payment to any new supplier the same way. Where amounts are significant, require two approvers. A message from a principal asking you to move money urgently and confidentially is the classic pattern, and the response is to telephone them on their known number and tell the family office regardless.

Credentials. Use a reputable password manager and long unique passwords, because the real danger is reuse: one breached service exposes every account sharing that password. Turn on multi factor authentication on email first, then banking, then everything else, preferring an authenticator application or a physical key to codes sent by text, which can be intercepted. Never share a login by message, and never run a household account that several people open with one password, because it destroys accountability. When anyone leaves, revoke access the same day across every system, including the shared calendars and delivery accounts everyone forgets.

Devices. Keep work and personal separate as far as the household allows. Encrypt every device, use a strong lock with a short timeout, and keep systems updated, since most successful intrusions exploit something already patched. Turn off lock screen message previews, because a locked phone on a table still shows an arriving message to anyone standing near it. Avoid public wireless for anything that matters, never plug in an unknown drive or charge from an unknown port, and lock your screen every time you stand up in a house where cleaners, contractors and guests move freely.

The home network and the smart house. A residence now holds dozens of connected devices and every one is a door. Change every default password, particularly on cameras, doorbells, routers and video recorders, since default credentials are published and scanned for automatically. Put the family on one network, guests on a separate guest network, and connected devices on a third, so a compromised device cannot reach a family laptop. Update firmware. Know who installed the system, who retains remote access, and remove any integrator's standing access no longer needed. Voice assistants listen for a wake word and record afterwards, so they belong in no study, bedroom or room where confidential conversations happen.

Cameras, and where they must never be. Cameras protect a perimeter. Inside a residence they create risk and liability in equal measure, and in most jurisdictions there are places they may not lawfully be: bathrooms, changing areas, bedrooms and staff private accommodation. Filming staff quarters is unlawful in many countries and indefensible everywhere. Where cameras exist, people must be informed, the legal basis and retention period are set by the family office rather than by you, access is restricted and logged, and audio is treated more strictly than video almost everywhere. A request to place a camera somewhere private goes to the family office and its legal advisers, not to an installer.

Photographs and metadata. Photographs carry embedded data including time, device and often exact location, and it travels with the file when the image is emailed or messaged. Before any image of a property leaves your hands, for an insurer, a contractor or a valuer, strip the metadata and read the frame: house numbers, gate mechanisms, alarm panels, safe doors, keys on a hook, a screen, a document, a view that identifies the address. The safest household rule is the simplest: photograph nothing of the family, the interiors or the vehicles, and post nothing about where you work, ever, including after you leave.

The group chat. Household group chats are efficient and they are the most common leak in private service. What belongs there: shift changes, tradespeople's arrival times, unremarkable logistics. What never belongs there: itineraries, flight and tail numbers, room numbers, guest names, alarm codes, health information, photographs of the family or interiors, financial details, and any opinion about a family member or colleague. Assume every message is read one day by a lawyer, a journalist or the person it concerns. Keep household communication on a platform the office has approved.

How it fails. It fails when a bank detail is changed on the strength of an email, or when a doorbell camera keeps the password it was sold with, or when a departed gardener's login is still live at Christmas. It fails when a phone on a hall table shows a preview to a contractor, or when a photograph for the insurer contains the alarm panel. And it fails whenever somebody was helpful very quickly.

Case I. The message from the principal. At six on a Friday a message arrives, apparently from the principal, from a number you do not recognise. They are in a meeting, cannot take calls, and need a deposit sent urgently to a new supplier. The tone is right and the message mentions a detail only an insider would know.

The debrief. Every element of that message is a technique: new number, unavailable for a call, urgency, confidentiality, and a true detail taken from research or a previous breach. Do not reply, because replying confirms the number is live and hands the sender your writing style. Telephone the principal on the number you already hold, and if they do not answer, telephone the family office or the chief of staff. Send nothing until you have spoken to a person on a number from your own records. If it proves genuine, nobody sensible will criticise the check. Report it either way, because your household is unlikely to be the only one receiving it.

Case II. The tablet in the kitchen. A new smart home system is installed. The integrator leaves a tablet in the kitchen, permanently logged in, with access to the cameras, door locks, alarm and calendar, and gives the household one password that everyone uses.

The debrief. Convenience has removed every trace of accountability, and anyone standing in the kitchen now controls the house. Raise it with the family office as a risk rather than a complaint, and bring the fix: individual accounts so actions are attributable, multi factor authentication on the administrator account, a short lock on the tablet, camera access limited to named people, the integrator's remote access removed or made time limited and logged, and default passwords changed everywhere. Ask who owns the system contractually and who is accountable for its security, because in many houses the answer is nobody. Shared credentials are the same thing as no credentials, and raising it in writing protects the household and also protects you.

The first hour after a suspected breach. Speed matters more than certainty, so act on suspicion. Report at once to the family office and whoever holds security, and do not investigate quietly alone. Delete nothing, since the message, the header and the log are the evidence. Disconnect an affected device from the network but leave it powered on unless told otherwise. If money has moved, telephone the bank immediately and ask for a recall, because the first hours are the only hours in which funds are commonly recoverable. Change passwords from a different, clean device. Write a timeline while it is fresh. Then let the specialists work. The instinct to fix it before anyone notices is what turns an incident into a disaster.

Your checklist, in any household. Is every change of payment detail verified by voice, on a number from your own records. Does every household email account carry multi factor authentication. Are there shared logins anywhere, and who else still has access who has left. Were the default passwords changed on the cameras, the router and the doorbell. Is there a camera anywhere it should not be. And is there anything in the household group chat this week that you would not want read aloud in a courtroom.

sections.h: Masterclass IX · Your Status, Pay & Mobility
sections.html:
This is the masterclass nobody teaches, and the one that decides whether a career in private service is sustainable or merely intense. The work is personal, the households are informal, and the money often arrives through arrangements nobody wrote down properly. Understanding your own position is not disloyalty. It is what lets you stay ten years instead of two. Nothing here is legal or tax advice, rules differ sharply between countries, and the answers belong to a qualified adviser in the relevant jurisdiction.

Employed or self employed, and why it decides everything. An employee works under direction, on set hours, integrated into the household, with equipment provided and no financial risk. A self employed contractor runs a business, serves several clients and carries the risk. Most authorities look at substance rather than label, so a household calling you self employed while directing your hours and forbidding other clients is probably wrong in law. Status carries notice, protection from unfair dismissal, paid holiday, sick pay and pension rights in most jurisdictions.

Who your legal employer actually is. Establish this in writing before you start. It may be a family member, a family office, a service company or an agency. That entity owes you notice and money and must be named if anything goes wrong, and it is rarely the person you serve daily. Ask under which law it employs you. If nobody can answer, that is information about how the household is run.

What must be in the contract. Insist on a written contract before you start. It should name the parties, the title and duties at a realistic scope, the start date, the places of work, salary with currency and payment date, hours, holiday, sick pay, notice on both sides, probation, accommodation, travel, confidentiality and the grievance route. Watch three clauses: duties so broad they mean anything, unlimited travel, and accommodation tied so tightly that losing the job means losing your home the same day. If a household resists a written contract, that is the most useful thing you will learn about them.

Notice, on both sides. Notice in senior private service commonly runs from one to three months, above statutory minimums a contract cannot undercut, though this varies by market. Check whether the household may pay in lieu and what happens to accommodation during notice. Probation usually carries less. Give the notice you agreed even when leaving is hard, because this industry is small and departures are discussed for years.

Live in accommodation. A live in role is a privilege and a complication. Establish whether the accommodation is provided for the better performance of the job, which in some jurisdictions affects how it is taxed and whether it counts as pay. Establish what happens when the employment ends, and negotiate a reasonable period to leave rather than the same afternoon. Get the practical points in writing: whether the space is genuinely private, whether visitors are permitted, and that nobody enters without notice. No camera or listening device belongs in staff accommodation, and in many countries it is unlawful there. Keep a home elsewhere if you can, because a role that is also your address concentrates too much in one relationship.

Hours and time off when you live where you work. The hardest thing about living in is that the job never visibly ends. Working time rules vary, and some countries exclude or partly exclude domestic work, so do not assume a protection you may not have. Define the week in the contract instead: which days are off and whether they are consecutive, what on call means and whether it is paid, how overtime is recognised, and what happens when the family travels and your rest days disappear. Then take the days you are given, in full, away from the house. Households rarely exploit people deliberately. They expand into whatever space is available.

Payroll and contributions. Insist on payslips showing gross, deductions and net, and check that tax and social contributions are actually paid rather than mentioned. Those contributions generate a state pension, unemployment cover and healthcare entitlement in many countries, and years paid in cash will not exist when you need them. Be wary of any arrangement where part of the salary is unrecorded cash, because the exposure sits with you as well as the household, and because it reduces what you can prove when you apply for a visa, a tenancy or a mortgage. Keep your own copies of every payslip and contract, permanently.

Tax residency when you follow a family. Residency rules differ by country and commonly turn on days present, on where your permanent home and centre of vital interests sit, and on tie breaker tests in double taxation treaties. Keep your own record of travel, stamps and boarding passes. It is entirely possible to become tax resident somewhere without intending it, and to owe filings in two countries in one year. Social security follows its own rules, separately from income tax. Take advice in the year you start moving rather than the year a letter arrives, and ask whether the family office will fund it, because a well run office usually will.

Visas and the right to work. The right to work is granted by a country rather than by an employer's enthusiasm, and a household that wants you cannot arrange it in a week. This constraint quietly decides which roles are open to you. Some countries operate routes for domestic workers accompanying an employer, usually tied to that employer and time limited. Others have no practical route for private household roles, which is why certain markets recruit almost entirely from people who already hold status. A tourist visa permits work nowhere, and accepting a role on one risks your immigration record, not the household's. Know the difference between a visa tied to one employer and one that lets you move, because the first shapes your leverage in every conversation.

References, in this world. References here are personal, spoken and cautious. Many principals will not write anything at all, for privacy rather than dissatisfaction, and some offices issue only a factual confirmation of dates and title. So build the evidence as you go: dated records of responsibilities, budgets managed, projects delivered and staff supervised, none of them confidential. Ask, while you are in the role and on good terms, who would speak to a future employer, and keep that relationship warm afterwards. And never trade on a former principal's name in public or in a profile, because the discretion you show about your last house is the only evidence anyone has of the discretion you will show about the next.

How it fails. It fails when three years pass on a verbal agreement and no record exists, or when part of the pay was cash and the mortgage application wants payslips. It fails when a role ends and the home ends the same afternoon, when nobody counted days spent in a country, or when someone on an employer tied visa stays in an unhappy house because leaving means leaving the country. And it fails when a person departs in anger and burns the only reference they had.

Case I. The offer with no paperwork. You are offered an excellent role. The family office says the contract will follow, that everyone starts this way, and that the first month will be paid against an invoice while payroll is arranged. They need you on Monday.

The debrief. You neither refuse nor simply accept. Reply warmly and in writing, setting out your understanding of the essentials, title, duties, salary, start date, hours, holiday and notice, and ask them to confirm it while the contract is drafted. That email is itself evidence if anything is later disputed. On the invoicing, ask whether the role is employed or self employed, because if it is employed then invoicing may misstate your status and the exposure lands on you. A well run office is never offended by a request to write things down, and one that is offended has told you something worth knowing.

Case II. The move you did not plan. The family announce that the household is relocating and that you will travel with them for most of the coming year, in long stretches across two other countries. Nobody has mentioned your contract, your visa or your tax position.

The debrief. Say yes to the principle and slow down the detail. Ask the office four questions in writing: which entity will employ you and under which law, what immigration status you will hold in each country and who is arranging it, how tax and social security will be handled and whether the office will fund advice, and how rest days work while travelling. None of it should sound like resistance; frame it as making sure you can travel with them lawfully and without an unpleasant surprise on either side. Start counting your days from the first flight. Mobility is the most valuable thing you have in this profession and the easiest to damage, and you protect it by asking early, in writing, in a friendly voice.

Your checklist, in any role. Do you have a written contract naming the legal employer. Are you employed or self employed, and does the reality match the label. Are payslips issued and contributions actually paid. If you live in, what happens to the accommodation when the role ends. How many days have you spent in each country this year. Is your right to work tied to this employer. And if you left tomorrow, who would speak for you, and have you asked them.

Money is where trust becomes measurable. A principal will forgive a late dinner and will never forgive an unexplained charge. You are not asked to be an accountant, you are asked to run a house on a known number and to leave a record clean enough that anyone could pick it up tomorrow. Nothing here is tax, legal or accounting advice, and where the sums are meaningful the family office and its advisors decide, not you.

The house is a business, the principal is not. The first discipline is the wall between the household operating budget, the cost of running a property and its staff, and the principal's personal spending, which is not your budget. Payroll, filters, pool chemicals, Tuesday's flowers, these are house. A watch, a school fee, a wire to a friend, these are personal and belong to the family office even when the request reaches your telephone. Houses come apart when both streams run through one card. Ask on day one which entity pays for the house, and if nobody has drawn the line, draw it in writing.

The annual budget, by category. A serious budget is built once a year in nine or ten defensible lines. Staff covers salaries, employer taxes, agency fees and overtime, and in most houses it is the largest by a distance. Maintenance covers service contracts, grounds, pool and repairs. Utilities is seasonal, not flat. Provisions covers food, beverage and consumables. Entertaining covers events, hired staff and florals, the most volatile line you hold. Vehicles covers insurance, servicing and fuel. Travel covers moving staff and readying houses. Professional fees covers bookkeeping, legal and appraisers. Capital projects sits apart, because a roof is not an operating cost and must never be buried in maintenance.

Variance, and the number that makes you speak. Variance is the gap between budget and spend. Read it two ways each month, the month against its own budget and the year to date against the year to date, because one heavy month is noise and a trend is a message. Agree a threshold in advance and write it down; a common shape is to flag any category running more than ten per cent or a few thousand dollars over, whichever comes first. The failure is never the overspend, it is the family office learning about it in December.

Petty cash, and why the float is the only sane method. Cash is a liability that looks like a convenience. Run it as an imprest float: a fixed sum, say one or two thousand dollars, in a locked box with one named custodian. Every disbursement leaves a receipt, so cash plus receipts always equals the float and anyone can count the box at any moment. When it runs low you submit the receipts, they are reimbursed exactly, and the box returns to its original figure, so there is no balance to reconstruct. Never lend from it, never pay staff from it.

The card protocol. The house card is issued in the name of the entity that owns the house, not the principal personally and not you, with a monthly and often a per transaction limit you did not set. Some things never touch it: cash advances, wages, anything for another household, anything personal to you even for an hour, any charge above your threshold without written sign off. The rule that saves you is the immediate receipt rule. Photograph the receipt at the counter before you leave and file it the same day with a category and a purpose.

The monthly close. Reconciliation is a rhythm, not a mood. Receipts filed daily, petty cash counted on the last working day, the statement matched line by line within three business days, invoices coded by the fifth, report out by the seventh. Matching line by line means every transaction has a receipt, a category and a purpose, and every receipt has a transaction. Anything unmatched goes on an exceptions list with the date you first chased it. A close that lands on the same date every month beats a perfect close that lands whenever you finished.

The one page report. Whoever receives your numbers, a controller, a chief financial officer, a business manager, wants the same shape. One page. A table of categories with four columns: budget, actual, variance, year to date variance. Below it, three to five bullets on the lines that moved, each one sentence with a cause and an action, for instance utilities up eleven per cent because irrigation ran the summer schedule early, timer revised. Then what is committed but unpaid, any decision you need with a date, and the exceptions.

The audit trail is your protection first. The paper you keep protects you before it protects them. When a question arrives eighteen months later, and it does, memory is worthless and the file is everything. Every payment has a document behind it, every approval above threshold exists as an email rather than a corridor conversation, every cash count carries two signatures, and nothing is altered afterwards without a dated note. Keep the records in the house system rather than a personal device, and never take a copy with you when you leave.

Approval thresholds, and how to get them written. A threshold is the figure above which you may not act alone. Most houses run tiers: you spend freely to a routine limit, seek written approval above it, and above a larger figure the family office or the principal signs. If nobody has given you the numbers, propose them in a short memo and ask for a reply confirming them. That reply is now your authority. Ask also for a named deputy, because a house with one signature stops on a Friday afternoon.

Deposits and advances. Money paid before work is money at risk. Keep deposits proportionate, tie each payment to a defined milestone rather than a date, and hold a final portion until the work is accepted. Log every deposit on a live schedule showing who holds it and when it is drawn down, because the deposit paid in March for a September party is invisible by August and is exactly the sum that disappears. Never advance cash, and never pay a deposit into a personal account when the invoice is from a company.

Gifts, tips and the end of the year. A great deal of household money moves as gratitude, and it moves through you. Client gifts, seasonal gifts to staff, tips to a crew after an event, the person who came out at two in the morning in January. All of it is spending and all of it is recorded: recipient, amount, occasion, who authorised it. Build the list in November and take one decision rather than fifteen in December. You never set the amounts alone, and you never accept a gift from a vendor whose invoices you approve.

House expense or personal benefit. This is the line that ends careers. A household card used for personal groceries, a house vendor sent to your own home, a case taken from the cellar, staff time borrowed for your move. People rationalise these as normal and none survive an audit. The rule has no exceptions: nothing paid for by the house benefits you unless it was granted in writing as part of your terms. Misappropriation in private service is rarely one theft, it is small blurred items nobody recorded, and the dismissal is for cause.

When you are asked to pay for something you do not understand. It will happen: a number, a name you do not recognise, an instruction to send funds quickly and quietly. Your posture is neither refusal nor compliance, it is process. You say you will action it, and you ask which entity and category it should be coded to and for the invoice. A legitimate request survives paperwork and an illegitimate one dissolves in it. If it arrives by email and is slightly unusual, verify by voice on a number you already hold, since impersonation of a principal is common. If pressure follows, escalate.

How it fails. It fails when one card pays for a house, a person and a second property. It fails when petty cash becomes a drawer with an approximate balance. It fails when receipts are gathered quarterly from a coat pocket. It fails when an eleven per cent overspend is absorbed silently for six months because you hoped it would even out. And it fails completely when there is no paper, because in the absence of a record the innocent explanation and the worst one weigh exactly the same.

Case I. The urgent wire. At four on a Friday a message from the principal's personal address asks you to send a substantial payment to a new consultant before the weekend, with an account number, no invoice, and a line saying they are in meetings and cannot take calls.

The debrief. Everything in that message is a pressure device: the hour, the urgency, the unreachability, the absence of paper. You do not refuse and you do not send. You reply that you will prepare it and need the invoice and the coding, and separately you call a number from your own records rather than any number in the message. If you cannot verify, the payment waits and you tell the family office why. A genuine principal will not dismiss you for verifying a wire.

Case II. The kitchen that grew. Six weeks into a refurbishment the contractor mentions cheerfully that the marble the principal admired in passing has been ordered, and the invoice arrives at nearly double the budgeted stone line. Nobody signed anything.

The debrief. This is a capital project, so it never belonged in maintenance, and a remark in a corridor is not an approval. Stop the payment, not the relationship. Write to the contractor the same day for a written change order showing original scope, change, price difference and schedule effect, then take it to the family office with your own note: budgeted, chosen, variance, recommendation. Then close the hole permanently by telling every active vendor in writing that no change is authorised without a countersigned change order.

Your checklist, before you touch a dollar. Do you know which entity pays for this house, in writing. Do you know your thresholds and your deputy approver. Is the house card in the entity's name with a limit you know. Is petty cash an imprest float with one custodian and a signed monthly count. Is there a fixed close date and a one page report going to a named person. Is there a live schedule of deposits paid and not yet drawn down. Can you produce the document behind any payment made this year.

sections.h: Masterclass XI · Vendors, Contracts and the Property
sections.html:
A house does not fail spectacularly. It fails through a gutter nobody cleared, a valve nobody labelled, a contractor nobody insured. The property is the largest asset you will ever hold the keys to, and the trades passing through it are strangers you invited, which makes vendor discipline and preventive maintenance one subject. Get it wrong and the consequence is a seven figure claim and a house uninhabitable for a year. Nothing here is legal or insurance advice, and specifics vary by state and by policy.

The black book, and what is actually in it. An amateur's vendor list is a set of telephone numbers. Yours holds, for each vendor: the legal and trading name, the licence number and expiry, the insurance certificate and expiry, the W-9 on file, a primary and a second contact, an after hours number a human answers, standard and callout rates, the scope on file, and one line of your own judgement on how they behave in a house. Add the field most people forget, who vetted them and when they were last background checked.

The scope of work, and the sentence that costs three months. A scope of work states exactly what will be done, with what materials, to what standard, by what date, for what price, and what is expressly excluded. The exclusions matter as much as the inclusions. A line such as refinish the floors as needed hands the vendor the definition of as needed and hands you the invoice. Write it as: these rooms, this many coats of this product, furniture moved and returned by the contractor, dust containment, these hours, price fixed. Then name the acceptance criteria, because finished is the word people argue about.

The W-9 before the first payment. No independent vendor is paid before a completed W-9 is on file. It carries the legal name, the business structure and the taxpayer identification number, and the entity needs it to issue the year end information return. Collect it at onboarding rather than at the moment of payment, because a vendor reluctant to provide one is telling you something worth hearing before they are inside the house. File it securely with restricted access.

The certificate of insurance, before anyone sets foot on the property. The COI is the most important paper in this masterclass and the rule has no exceptions, including for the friend of the family doing it as a favour. Read it rather than filing it. Check that the named insured matches the legal entity on your contract letter for letter, because cover issued to a similarly named company is no cover at all. Check general liability, the per occurrence limit and the aggregate, which for meaningful work on a significant residence commonly means a million or more per occurrence. Check workers compensation, which stands between the household and a personal claim when someone falls off a ladder. Check the validity dates against the dates of the work, and check the household entity is listed as additional insured, because otherwise the policy protects the contractor and not you.

Employee or independent contractor. This is not pedantry, it is one of the largest exposures in private service. Broadly, you direct an employee's work, hours and method and supply the tools, while an independent contractor controls how the work is done, brings their own tools and insurance and bears business risk. A housekeeper working set hours under your direction looks like an employee whatever anyone calls them, and treating that person as a contractor can produce back taxes and penalties for the household. Your job is to see the risk and get counsel to decide it in writing.

Change orders, never verbally. A change order is a written amendment to the scope, priced and dated, describing the change, the cost, the schedule effect and who approved it, signed by both sides before the work is done. Verbal changes are how a project doubles: the contractor remembers approval, you remember a discussion, and neither is lying. Tell every vendor at the outset, in writing, that no verbal instruction from anyone in the household constitutes authorisation, including from the principal in passing, and that unauthorised work will not be paid.

Liens, waivers and retainage. In most of the United States an unpaid contractor, subcontractor or supplier can file a mechanic's lien against the property itself, clouding title long after the dispute is forgotten. The frightening version: the general contractor is paid in full, fails to pay a subcontractor, and the subcontractor liens the house. Your defence is the lien waiver, exchanged against every payment, conditional or unconditional, partial or final, and you never release an unconditional waiver against a cheque that has not settled. Separately hold retainage, commonly five to ten per cent, until the punch list closes.

Access to the property. Decide the rules before the first van arrives and never improvise them at the gate. Every worker is on a list submitted in advance with a full legal name, checked against identification on arrival and issued a dated badge surrendered on departure. Fix the hours and the route: service entrance, one bathroom, one break area, rooms that are out of bounds. Escort any trade into a private area, always. Certain things never happen: no photography, no gate or alarm codes given to a vendor, no key released without a signed log, no substitute worker admitted.

The preventive maintenance calendar, and its real cadence. A house is a machine and a machine has a schedule. Monthly: air handling filters, generator tested under load, smoke and carbon monoxide alarms. Quarterly: water treatment, sump pumps checked by lifting the float, plumbing under every sink inspected for the small dark stain that precedes a flood. Twice a year: heating commissioned before the cold and cooling before the heat, gutters cleared in late spring and after leaf fall, irrigation blown out before the freeze. Annually: chimneys swept before first use, roof and flashings surveyed by someone who actually goes up, extinguishers certified. A calendar without records is a wish list.

Water, which is the enemy. Every member of staff without exception must be able to walk to the main water shut off in the dark and close it. Label it, photograph it, put it in the house manual, and walk each new person to it on their first day along with the isolation valves under sinks and for the irrigation. Then install what insurers now expect on a significant residence: leak sensors at every water heater, under every sink, behind the washing machine, in the mechanical room and at the base of each riser, tied to an automatic shut off valve on the main that closes when a sensor triggers or when flow continues abnormally in an empty house. Confirm the alerts reach a phone someone answers at three in the morning. A supply line that fails in an empty house in January runs for days, destroying millwork, plaster, art and structure. That is the incident that ends careers.

Power, generators and the panel. The standby generator gets a monthly exercise under load, not merely a start, because a generator that runs unloaded for years fails on the night it is needed. Confirm the transfer switch actually transfers, keep the fuel full and treated, and hold an annual service contract. Know where the main and secondary electrical panels are and have every breaker legibly labelled with the room it serves, verified by testing rather than by a previous era's marker pen. Know which circuits the generator picks up, because that is the difference between an inconvenient night and a burst pipe.

Closing a house for the season. Emptying a house is a written protocol and you follow the same sheet every time. Water off at the main and the system drained, or the heating left on with a low temperature alarm set, depending on climate. Water heaters and irrigation drained where there is freeze risk. Refrigerators emptied, perishables gone, bins emptied so nothing invites pests. Alarm armed in unoccupied mode with the monitoring company told the property is vacant. Leak and temperature sensors confirmed live. A named person walking the property weekly with a photographic report. And check the vacancy clause in the policy, since many restrict cover beyond a stated number of days.

What happens during an outage. When the power goes you work a sequence rather than a panic. Confirm whether it is the property or the area, report it and take a reference number and an estimate. Verify the generator picked up and that the expected circuits are live. Check sump pumps, freezers, any medical equipment, and the network, since alarm and cameras may depend on it. Protect the water system against freezing if the heating is not on the generator. Communicate once, clearly, and write the timeline as it happens, because the insurer will ask.

How it fails. It fails when a favour is done by an uninsured friend of the gardener who then falls. It fails when a scope says as needed. It fails when a change is agreed in a corridor and invoiced in a letter. It fails when a general contractor is paid in full and a subcontractor liens the title. And it fails most expensively when a house is closed for four months, nobody walks it, a supply line lets go in week two, and the first person to know opens the door in spring.

Case I. The favour at the gate. A tree crew is due Tuesday. On Monday the principal mentions that a neighbour recommended someone cheaper and faster, and that person is at the gate now with two workers and a chainsaw, offering to start immediately.

The debrief. The gate stays shut, courteously. You ask for the company name, the licence and a certificate showing general liability and workers compensation with the household entity as additional insured, and you explain, to the principal rather than to the crew, that this takes an hour and not a week. Tree work is among the highest injury trades there is, and an uninsured worker hurt on the property becomes a claim against the household directly. If the paperwork is clean by the afternoon they work, and if not the Tuesday crew comes as planned.

Case II. The house nobody walked. The family leaves the mountain house in October and returns in May. You inherit the post in January and find a signed closing checklist in the manual, but no walk through reports and no record of who holds the keys.

The debrief. A signed checklist tells you what someone intended in October, not what the house is doing in January, so treat it as unverified. Get someone qualified inside this week with a camera: temperature in every zone, the main valve position, the leak sensors and their batteries, the heating, the alarm status, and photographs of every mechanical space and ceiling. Then build what was missing, a weekly inspection with a dated photographic report, monitored leak and low temperature alerts reaching an answered phone, and a key log.

Your checklist, for the property and everyone who enters it. Does every active vendor hold a current COI naming the entity as additional insured, with the expiry diaried. Is there a W-9 on file before the first payment. Is there a written scope for every job above your threshold. Have you told every vendor in writing that verbal change orders are not authorised. Are you exchanging lien waivers against payment and holding retainage. Can every member of staff find the main water shut off in the dark. Are there leak sensors tied to an automatic shut off. Has the generator run under load this month.

books.title: The Casebook
books.subtitle: Real situations from the private-service world, with the wrong moves, the professional's move, and the principle beneath each one.
books.intro: You will not be tested on what you can recite. You will be tested, in a real house, on what you do in the ten seconds after something goes wrong. This casebook trains exactly that. Read each case, decide your move before you read the answer, and let the principles become instinct.

sections.h: Case 1 · The wine on the Birkin
sections.html:
The situation. At a private dinner, a guest reaches across the table and knocks a full glass of red wine over the principal's cream Birkin, resting on the empty chair beside her. The table goes quiet. The guest is mortified. The principal glances at you.

The wrong moves. To gasp or react to the damage. To fuss over the bag at the table and prolong the moment. To reassure the guest loudly, which only deepens their embarrassment and draws more attention.

The professional's move. You are beside the principal within seconds, calm and unhurried. You lift the bag away discreetly, murmur that it will be taken care of, and immediately turn the room's attention back to the evening: a fresh glass, a quiet word, the service flowing again. The bag goes at once to a specialist leather cleaner from your black book; you never attempt to treat it yourself. The next morning you report to the principal what has been done, not what went wrong.

The principle. In a crisis at the table, the object is never the priority; the atmosphere is. You protect the evening first, the belonging second, and you make the accident disappear so smoothly that within a minute it is as if it never happened.

The situation. You discover that the principal has agreed to be in two places on Thursday, a board meeting in Geneva at 9am and a family lunch in London at 1pm, and only one aircraft is available. Neither commitment has been cancelled. Neither party knows about the other.

The wrong moves. To wait and hope it resolves itself. To cancel the one you personally judge less important. To present the clash to the principal as a problem, with no solution attached.

The professional's move. You confirm the hard constraints quietly (aircraft availability, flight times, ground transfers), then you build two or three viable options: a repositioning that makes both possible, a later lunch, a second aircraft chartered for the gap. You bring the clash and the solutions to the principal together, in one calm sentence: "There is an overlap on Thursday. Here are two ways it works, and my recommendation."

The principle. Never surface a problem without a path through it. Anticipation is catching the clash early; professionalism is arriving with the solution already in hand, so the principal only ever has to choose.

sections.h: Case 3 · The journalist at the gate
sections.html:
The situation. A polite, well-dressed person appears at the residence, says they are writing a warm profile of the family, and asks you to confirm just one small, harmless-sounding detail. They are friendly, and they are not going away.

The wrong moves. To confirm the "harmless" detail. To deny it (a denial is still information). To engage in conversation at all, however pleasant.

The professional's move. You are courteous and completely closed. You neither confirm nor deny anything, you offer nothing, and you end the exchange warmly but firmly. You then report the approach immediately to the family office and the security detail, with the time, the description and what was asked.

The principle. A journalist counts on a friendly slip. Discretion here is not silence about secrets, it is refusing to be a source at all, and treating even the smallest detail as sacred. What you must not do is the one thing that feels most natural: be helpful.

sections.h: Case 4 · The housekeeper and the chef
sections.html:
The situation. Two long-serving members of staff, the housekeeper and the private chef, are in open conflict. The tension is affecting the whole house. Each comes to you privately, certain they are right, and each expects you to take their side.

The wrong moves. To take a side to end it quickly. To ignore it and hope it passes. To carry each one's complaints to the other, which pours fuel on the fire.

The professional's move. You listen to each, fully and without judgement, and you commit to nothing in the moment. You address the practical friction (whose station is whose, how the kitchen and the house intersect, timings) rather than the personalities. You speak to them together only once you understand both sides, and you frame it as the smooth running of the house, not as a verdict on who is right.

The principle. Harmony among staff is itself a service to the principal. Your job is not to win the argument for someone, it is to remove the friction and restore the calm, while keeping the respect of both. Alliances built on taking sides always curdle.

sections.h: Case 5 · The outburst that is not about you
sections.html:
The situation. The principal snaps at you sharply over something small, a car that is early, a coffee a shade too cool, in a tone far beyond the moment. You have done nothing wrong.

The wrong moves. To defend yourself and explain. To take it personally and go cold. To match the emotion.

The professional's move. You absorb it calmly, fix the small thing without a word of defence, and read what is underneath. Very often the sharpness is anxiety about something ahead, and it has simply landed on you. You quietly ease whatever is really weighing on the day, and you let the moment pass without making them apologise for it.

The principle. Read the emotion beneath the words, and solve the feeling, not just the sentence. Taking it personally is the rookie error; the professional understands that they are, in that moment, the safest place for the principal to put a difficult feeling, and they carry it without flinching.

sections.h: Case 6 · The secret a colleague "already knows"
sections.html:
The situation. The principal told you something in confidence weeks ago. Now another member of staff hints, knowingly, that they know it too, and waits for you to confirm.

The wrong moves. To confirm it, since they "obviously already know". To ask them who told them. To look flustered, which confirms it anyway.

The professional's move. You neither confirm nor deny. You stay warm and unreadable, you hold exactly what you were told, and you let the hint fall flat. If the leak is significant, you note it, but you do not become the second source.

The principle. A confidence does not lapse because someone else guessed. "They already know" is the oldest trap in the house, and the professional never walks into it. What you were told in trust stays with you, whatever anyone else appears to know.

sections.h: Case 7 · The vendor who offers you a cut
sections.html:
The situation. A supplier you use for the household quietly offers you a personal commission to keep sending them the family's business, or you discover the household is being overcharged by a vendor connected to another member of staff.

The wrong moves. To accept the arrangement, "since everyone does it". To confront the vendor or the colleague dramatically. To say nothing and let it continue.

The professional's move. You decline any personal benefit, immediately and without ambiguity. Where the household is being overcharged, you gather the facts quietly, then raise it cleanly with the family office or principal, framed as protecting the household's interests, not as accusing a colleague. You let the structure handle the person.

The principle. Your integrity around the household's money is the fastest way to become indispensable, and the fastest way to lose everything if it slips. You never take a cut, and you protect the principal's purse as if it were a confidence, because it is one.

The situation. You dropped a ball. A booking missed, a valuable chipped, an instruction misread. Only you know, and it would be easy to say nothing.

The wrong moves. To hide it and hope. To take forty minutes explaining how it happened. To over-apologise until the principal has to reassure you.

The professional's move. You take four minutes, not forty: fact, fix, ownership. You state plainly what happened, you present the solution already underway, you own it without excuses, and you move on. You do not relitigate it, and you do not let it become a performance of guilt.

The principle. Trust survives an honest mistake; it rarely survives a hidden one. Hiding an error is the single failure that almost never survives discovery. Fact, fix, ownership, and then let it go, is the whole of professional recovery.

Do not read it once. Come back to a case the week before you start a role, and again whenever a real situation echoes one here. In time, the move will arrive before the thought does, and that is the point. A household does not keep the person who knows the theory. It keeps the person who, in the ten seconds after something goes wrong, simply does the right thing.

Maison Amarine · The Private Concierge Academy · Confidential, for the use of our mentees only.

books.title: Cultural Fluency
books.subtitle: The codes of service change with the family you serve. This is the map that keeps you from a quiet, unforgettable mistake.
books.intro: A note before anything else: what follows are patterns, not rules. Wealth is global and individual, and a family will always override the tendencies of the culture they come from. Read the person, never the flag. But knowing these patterns means you arrive prepared, you avoid the error that is never mentioned and never forgotten, and you show, without a word, that you understand the world you are entering.

sections.html: Before you serve any family, do three things. Learn, discreetly, where they are from and how they live. Observe, in your first days, more than you act. And when in doubt, ask a trusted colleague or the family office quietly, rather than assume. Cultural fluency is not performing knowledge of a culture; it is the humility to notice, and the care to get the small things right.

The spirit. Hospitality is close to sacred. A guest is honoured generously, and the household is expected to reflect that same warmth and abundance. Generosity, dignity and respect for hierarchy run through everything.

What it means for your service.

  • Religion shapes the rhythm. Prayer times, Ramadan (when the household's hours and meals shift entirely, fasting by day and gathering by night), and the significance of Friday. Food is halal; alcohol, where present at all, is handled with discretion and never assumed.
  • Coffee and dates (gahwa) are a ritual of welcome, offered promptly and correctly. Learn the form.
  • Modesty and privacy, particularly around the women of the family, are treated with great care. Discretion about the household is absolute.
  • Time has a different texture. Plans can shift; patience and grace matter more than rigid punctuality, and generosity of spirit is read as respect.
  • Gifts and gestures carry weight; understand their meaning before you give or receive.

The reflex. Lead with warmth, respect the faith and its rhythms without ever being asked to, and treat the family's privacy as the highest duty.

The spirit. Warm informality laid over very high standards. The register is friendly and direct, but the expectation of excellence is total, and efficiency is prized.

What it means for your service.

  • Informality is a style, not a lowering of standards. You may be on first-name terms and spoken to as a valued professional, yet the bar for delivery is as high as anywhere.
  • Directness and speed. Clear communication, fast execution, solutions over process. A problem is expected to arrive with its answer.
  • Wellness and longevity are central: trainers, nutritionists, recovery, biohacking, the best doctors. This is a serious part of the modern American household.
  • Philanthropy is often woven into the family's identity and calendar; galas, foundations, causes.
  • Tipping and gratuity norms are their own culture; learn them per setting, because getting them wrong is quietly noticed.

The reflex. Be warm, be fast, be flawless. Match the easy tone without ever easing the standard beneath it.

The spirit. Understatement, heritage and restraint. The oldest wealth rarely announces itself; it is signalled by what is quiet, worn-in and inherited rather than by what is new and loud.

What it means for your service.

  • Discretion and formality are the default. Protocol, titles where they apply, and correct etiquette matter, and are noticed when absent.
  • Patina over polish. The family treasures the aged silver, the worn leather, the inherited piece. You never over-restore an heirloom, and you never mistake the understated for the unimportant.
  • Restraint in language. The loud superlative, the word "iconic", the visible price, all read as new money. The register is quiet.
  • Continuity. Long-standing suppliers, traditions, the way things have always been done. You honour the house's memory before you improve it.

The reflex. Quiet, precise, correct. Let nothing you do be loud, and treat the heritage of the house as something to protect rather than modernise.

The spirit. Diverse and not one culture, but with recurring themes of hierarchy, face, family and meticulous respect. A Japanese household and a Chinese one differ greatly; learn the specific family, always.

What it means for your service.

  • Face and respect. Never cause anyone to lose face, especially in front of others. Corrections and difficulties are handled privately and gently.
  • Hierarchy and the elders. Seniority is honoured carefully, in seating, in address, in the order of service.
  • Gift etiquette is precise. How a gift is wrapped, presented (often with both hands) and received matters; certain numbers and colours carry meaning. Learn them before an occasion, not during it.
  • Japanese omotenashi: a philosophy of anticipatory, selfless hospitality that expects the need to be met before it is spoken, and the effort to be invisible. It is, in many ways, the purest expression of this entire craft.
  • Family and business are often deeply intertwined; discretion spans both.

The reflex. Observe closely, honour seniority, protect everyone's face, and treat the details of ritual, the gift, the greeting, the order, as things worth getting exactly right.

Beneath every culture, the deepest codes are the same. Discretion is prized everywhere. Anticipation is valued everywhere. Respect, warmth and reliability translate into every language. A family from any corner of the world wants the same thing at heart: to feel that their private life is safe in your hands, arranged with care, and never remarked upon.

So learn the patterns, prepare for the family, and then do the one thing that works in every house on earth: read the person in front of you, and serve them as they wish to be served, not as a book, not even this one, told you they would.

Maison Amarine · The Private Concierge Academy · Confidential, for the use of our mentees only.
